src/components/ui/CollapsibleLegend.tsx.title: Legend drag handle is a non-keyboard div; toggle lacks aria-expanded
src/components/ui/CollapsibleLegend.tsx.detail: The reposition handle `<div onMouseDown={handleMouseDown} className="... cursor-grab ..." title="Drag to move">` (lines 87-97) is a div with only a mouse handler — no role/tabIndex/keyboard alternative, so keyboard users cannot move the legend (WCAG 2.1.1). The expand/collapse button (lines 98-121) also has no aria-expanded. Lower severity: panel is desktop-only (`hidden md:block`, line 79) and repositioning is non-essential.
src/components/ui/CollapsibleLegend.tsx.fix: Add aria-expanded={expanded} to the toggle button. For the drag handle, either provide a keyboard reposition affordance or mark it aria-hidden with an accessible note that dragging is mouse-only; at minimum it should not be a focus dead-end.

src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.title: Silent swallow of incoming data with a misleading 'parse' comment; no message-shape guard at the wrapper boundary
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.detail: Lines 47-55: `this.connection.on('data', (data) => { try { const message = data as MultiplayerMessage; this.handleMessage(message); } catch { /* Failed to parse message - ignore */ } })`. The comment is wrong — PeerJS already deserialized the object, so nothing is being parsed; the try/catch silently swallows ANY error thrown downstream in handleMessage AND the entire MultiplayerManager.handleMessage chain (invoked via the onMessage callback at :94). That means real bugs/exceptions in message handling vanish with no log. The cast `data as MultiplayerMessage` also provides no runtime safety. This is the boundary where a `typeof message?.type === 'string'` guard belongs.
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.fix: Replace the bare cast with a minimal runtime guard: if (!message || typeof (message as { type?: unknown }).type !== 'string') return; then handleMessage. In the catch, log via logger.multiplayer.warn with the error instead of swallowing, and correct the comment to 'handler error' not 'parse'.

src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.title: Untrusted peer DataChannel payload passed to handlers with zero validation
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.detail: conn.on('data', (data) => { ... const message = data as MultiplayerMessage; this.handleMessage(message); }). The incoming WebRTC data from an untrusted peer is type-asserted (`as MultiplayerMessage`) and dispatched directly. The empty catch only guards a synchronous throw that never happens here. By contrast WebSocketAdapter.handleMessage validates via isValidWSMessage() before use. A malicious peer can send `{type:'PLAYER_JOIN', payload:{...}}` with arbitrary id/name/color, or any message with a missing/malformed payload, and it flows straight into MultiplayerManager.handleMessage -> Zustand store mutations (store.addRemotePlayer, store.setMachineLock, store.resolveIntent, store.addChatMessage). message.payload.timestamp in the PING handler is dereferenced unchecked (line 79) - a PING with no payload throws and is silently swallowed, but the real risk is the unvalidated objects reaching store mutators.
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.fix: Add a validateMultiplayerMessage(data): data is MultiplayerMessage type-guard (mirroring messageValidation.ts/isValidWSMessage): check data is an object, type is one of the known string literals, and the payload shape matches per-type (e.g. PLAYER_JOIN requires id:string,name:string,color in PLAYER_COLORS). Reject (return without dispatch, increment an error counter) on failure. Wire it at the top of PeerConnection.handleMessage before any branch.

src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.title: No inbound message-size or rate bound on WebRTC DataChannel (untrusted-peer DoS)
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.detail: PeerConnection has no cap on the size or frequency of incoming messages. A malicious peer can flood the host with FULL_STATE_SYNC/CHAT/PLAYER_UPDATE messages or a single huge payload (e.g. CHAT.message of many MB, or a STATE_SYNC with a giant machines array), all of which are dispatched into Zustand store mutations and, for CHAT, accumulated in addChatMessage. There is no per-peer message-rate throttle or byte-length guard. Combined with the validation gap above, this is a cheap client-side DoS / memory-exhaustion vector.
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.fix: In PeerConnection.handleMessage, reject messages whose serialized JSON length exceeds a sane cap (e.g. 64KB for control msgs); enforce a max CHAT.message length (e.g. 500 chars) in validation; add a simple per-peer token-bucket rate limit (drop+count when exceeded). Cap chat history length in store.addChatMessage.

src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.title: Connection metadata trusted for playerId without validation (disconnect/eviction targeting)
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.detail: getMetadata() returns the raw conn.metadata cast to Record<string,unknown>. MultiplayerManager.handlePeerDisconnected (line 251-255) reads metadata.playerId and uses it (when host) as the player id to removeRemotePlayer/removePeer and broadcast PLAYER_LEAVE. metadata is fully controlled by the connecting peer (set client-side in SignalingService.connectToHost metadata, lines 122-125). A guest can set metadata.playerId to another player's id so that, on its own disconnect, the host evicts the victim instead.
src/multiplayer/PeerConnection.ts.fix: On the host, map peerId->playerId at verified join time and use that map for disconnect cleanup; never trust metadata.playerId for destructive store operations. Treat metadata as a display hint only.

src/utils/geminiClient.ts.title: Context-overflow detection relies on brittle English substring matching of error messages
src/utils/geminiClient.ts.detail: isContextOverflowError (geminiClient.ts:256-261) matches error.message.toLowerCase() against a hardcoded English phrase list (CONTEXT_OVERFLOW_PATTERNS at :30-37: 'context length','token limit','too long', etc.). The legacy SDK's actual overflow errors carry structured status codes (e.g. INVALID_ARGUMENT) and provider-specific wording that may not contain any of these phrases. When it misses, the overflow is counted as a normal failure (recordFailure at :317), needlessly advancing the circuit breaker toward open instead of triggering the intended heuristic fallback (:310-314). Low because truncation at :293 already caps prompt size, making real overflows rare.
src/utils/geminiClient.ts.fix: Prefer matching on the SDK error's status/code (e.g. err.status === 'INVALID_ARGUMENT' or HTTP 400 with a token-limit reason) and keep the substring list only as a secondary heuristic.

src/utils/geminiClient.ts.title: API key retained on GeminiClient instance and never cleared from response cache scope
src/utils/geminiClient.ts.detail: initialize() stores `this.apiKey = apiKey` (line 130). disconnect() nulls it (line 177), which is correct. The key is never logged (getMaskedApiKey masks correctly), so no leak via logger. The residual concern is only that the in-memory plaintext key plus the persisted localStorage copy (aiConfigStore.ts:558) means two live plaintext copies; clearSensitiveFields() exists for exactly this but is unused. Lower confidence because JS gives no real memory-zeroing guarantee anyway and disconnect already nulls the reference.
src/utils/geminiClient.ts.fix: No code change strictly required; if hardening, route key clearing through clearSensitiveFields in disconnect() for consistency. Primarily reinforces the plaintext-localStorage finding above — fix that one first.

package.json.title: PeerJS 1.5.5 + default public signaling server: untrusted-peer trust boundary unhardened
package.json.detail: peerjs ^1.5.5 (package.json:38, resolved 1.5.5) connects via the default public PeerJS cloud server (src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts:26-30) with predictable host peer IDs derived from the room code ('millos-${roomCode}', SignalingService.ts:53-55). Predictable IDs let any third party on the public broker impersonate or pre-claim a host ID / squat room codes, and the broad 'wss:' CSP (see separate finding) does nothing to constrain the broker. This is an inherent property of using the shared public broker with deterministic IDs, not a code bug, but it is a real multiplayer trust-boundary weakness for a feature that explicitly accepts untrusted peers.
package.json.fix: Add entropy to host peer IDs (append a random nonce shared out-of-band with the room code) so IDs are unguessable, and/or self-host peerjs-server (referenced at SignalingService.ts:28) with the connect-src locked to that host. Validate/authorize incoming DataConnections at the application layer before trusting peer-sent state. Product decision (changes multiplayer UX/join flow).

package.json.title: Version inconsistency: package.json 0.20.0 vs deployed v0.30 'current'
package.json.detail: package.json declares `"version": "0.20.0"` (line 4), but deploy.yml builds and serves v0.30 as the current/default version (`VERSION=v0.30 npm run build`, root redirects to /v0.30/). metadata.json carries no version. The shipped 'current' is v0.30 while the package manifest says 0.20.0 — version of record disagrees with what users see. Git tags are also inconsistent (v0.10.0, v0.2, v0.3 — mixed formats).
package.json.fix: Bump package.json version to 0.30.0 to match the deployed current build, and standardize tag format. Low-risk metadata change.

src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.title: DRACO JS decoder executed as third-party code from www.gstatic.com (remote-code dependency)
src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.detail: DRACO_DECODER_PATH = 'https://www.gstatic.com/draco/versioned/decoders/1.5.7/' (line 33) with setDecoderConfig({ type: 'js' }) (line 48). Three's DRACOLoader fetches draco_decoder.js / draco_wasm_wrapper.js and runs them inside a blob Worker. Although the Worker runs from a blob (allowed by worker-src 'self' blob:), the executed code is fetched at runtime from a Google-controlled CDN and is not subresource-integrity-pinned. A compromise or version change at gstatic would execute attacker-controlled JS in the worker context. This is a deliberate trade-off (offline-fallback path to '/draco/' is commented at line 36), but for launch it pins integrity to Google's CDN with no SRI.
src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.fix: Vendor the DRACO 1.5.7 decoder into public/draco/ and switch DRACO_DECODER_PATH to '/draco/' (the commented local path), removing the runtime third-party code fetch entirely and fixing the CSP gap in the same stroke. This also makes DRACO models work offline via the existing SW MODEL_CACHE. Behavior-neutral if the local decoder matches the bundled three version.

src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.title: DRACO decoder loaded from gstatic CDN at runtime — cross-origin dep + offline-cache miss + possible CSP block
src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.detail: Line 33: `const DRACO_DECODER_PATH = 'https://www.gstatic.com/draco/versioned/decoders/1.5.7/';`, set at line 47, preload()ed at line 49. forklift.glb (1.6M) is the only DRACO model. This adds a cross-origin fetch to gstatic on first model load, is NOT in SW MODEL_CACHE (cross-origin, breaks offline), and gstatic.com is NOT in index.html CSP connect-src (only cdn.jsdelivr.net + fonts.gstatic.com listed) so the decoder fetch may be CSP-blocked in production. Local fallback path commented out at line 36.
src/utils/dracoLoader.ts.fix: Vendor the DRACO decoder into public/draco/ and set DRACO_DECODER_PATH = `${import.meta.env.BASE_URL}draco/` so it is same-origin, SW-cacheable, CSP-allowed, offline-capable.

src/utils/serviceWorkerRegistration.ts.title: SW message handler trusts any message origin/shape (no source/type validation)
src/utils/serviceWorkerRegistration.ts.detail: In public/sw.js the message handler (lines 231-247) destructures event.data.type without verifying event.origin or that event.data is an object, and replies on event.ports[0] for CLEAR_CACHE and GET_CACHE_SIZE. Any script in any client controlled by this SW (same-origin only, so impact is bounded) can clear all millos- caches or enumerate cached URL pathnames (getCacheStats returns full URL pathnames, lines 259-262). For a same-origin-only app this is low severity, but a successful same-origin XSS gains a cache-wipe / cache-enumeration primitive for free.
src/utils/serviceWorkerRegistration.ts.fix: Guard the handler: 'if (!event.data || typeof event.data !== "object") return;' and validate type against an allowlist before acting. Optionally restrict GET_CACHE_SIZE detail (drop full pathnames, return counts only). Low-risk hardening, no UX change.

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: PeerJS debug uses process.env.NODE_ENV in a Vite/browser context (likely undefined → never debug, minor)
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: PEERJS_CONFIG.debug = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? 2 : 0 (line 29). Vite exposes import.meta.env, not process.env, in browser code; process.env.NODE_ENV is statically replaced by Vite's define only if configured, otherwise it is undefined at runtime. Practically this means debug is always 0 (the safe branch), so there is no information-leak risk — but it is a latent correctness smell indicating the env check does not do what it reads as, and if a future define exposes process.env in prod it could flip verbose PeerJS logging on. Not a security defect today; flagged for the env-API mismatch.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Use import.meta.env.DEV (consistent with serviceWorkerRegistration.ts:45) instead of process.env.NODE_ENV for the debug gate. Behavior-neutral today, removes the latent footgun.

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: WebRTC exposes every peer's public IP address to untrusted peers with no consent or disclosure
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: PEERJS_CONFIG passes no `config`/`iceServers`, so PeerJS falls back to its built-in default ICE servers (third-party): `const PEERJS_CONFIG = { debug: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? 2 : 0 };`. WebRTC connection establishment inherently exchanges ICE candidates that reveal each participant's public IP address (often local network IPs too) to every other peer in the room. The task states peers are untrusted, so every participant learns every other participant's IP. An IP address is personal data under GDPR (Recital 30 / Breyer C-582/14). It is disclosed with zero consent flow, zero disclosure, and no privacy policy anywhere in the app. This is the strongest GDPR exposure and does not depend on PeerJS broker internals.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Owner decision required. Mitigations: (a) display a clear pre-join disclosure in MultiplayerLobby that joining a room reveals your IP address to other participants and routes signaling through a third-party broker; (b) optionally configure a TURN relay with `iceTransportPolicy: 'relay'` to hide peer IPs (cost/perf tradeoff); (c) publish a privacy policy covering this. Do not hardcode a specific STUN host as a 'fact'.

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: Signaling (peer IDs embedding room code + player name/id metadata) routed through default PeerJS cloud broker, a third party, undisclosed
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: The default PeerJS cloud broker (no custom `host`/`path`/`key` in PEERJS_CONFIG; comment: 'Uses default PeerJS cloud server (free tier...)') handles signaling. What touches this third party is: (1) the peer IDs, which embed the room code in cleartext — `const peerId = this.config.isHost ? \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}\` : \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}-${this.config.playerId}\`;` (lines 53-55); and (2) the connection metadata `{ playerName: this.config.playerName, playerId: this.config.playerId }` sent at connect time (lines 122-125). Note: chat messages and player positions travel peer-to-peer over the encrypted DataChannel and do NOT pass through the broker — only signaling/handshake data does. Still, player name + room code reaching an undisclosed third-party processor with no DPA, consent, or notice is a GDPR transparency/lawful-basis gap.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Owner decision: disclose the third-party broker in a privacy notice, and/or self-host peerjs-server (link already in the file comment) to keep signaling under the operator's control. Do not transmit the player name as broker metadata if it can be exchanged post-connection over the DataChannel instead.

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: Guessable 6-char room code + auto-FULL_STATE_SYNC discloses all players' names/colors to an uninvited peer
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: The host peer ID is deterministic: `const hostPeerId = \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}\`;` where roomCode is a 6-char code from a 32-symbol alphabet (multiplayerStore.generateRoomCode). Anyone who guesses or enumerates a code connects to the host, and on connection the host immediately sends FULL_STATE_SYNC and then loops over every existing remote player broadcasting their name and color to the newcomer: `peerConn.send({ type: 'PLAYER_JOIN', payload: { id: existingPlayer.id, name: existingPlayer.name, color: existingPlayer.color } });` (MultiplayerManager.ts:214-225). So an uninvited peer receives the PII (display names) of everyone already in the room. No rate limiting, no room password, no host approval.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Owner decision (UX/behavior change): add a host approval gate before sending PLAYER_JOIN/FULL_STATE_SYNC to a new connection, and/or a longer/secret room code or join PIN. Do not broadcast existing players' names to a peer until the host accepts them.

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: No consent gate: host auto-accepts any incoming peer and existing players never approve new participants
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: handleIncomingConnection accepts every inbound connection with no gate: `private handleIncomingConnection(conn: DataConnection): void { const peerId = conn.peer; ...; this.setupConnectionHandlers(conn, peerId); }`. The host then broadcasts the newcomer's name to all other peers (MultiplayerManager.ts:228-238). Existing participants neither consent to, nor can refuse, a specific new peer learning their display name and receiving their avatar/position stream. There is no UI anywhere informing a user that entering a name and joining will share that name and live position with arbitrary other participants.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Owner decision (UX/behavior): add an approval prompt for incoming peers and/or a one-line disclosure at name entry in MultiplayerLobby ('Your name, chat, and in-game position will be shared with other players in this room.').

src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.title: Host peer-id squatting: predictable id enables room hijack/denial before host registers
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.detail: Host peer-id is the deterministic `millos-${roomCode}` (line 54). An attacker who learns/guesses a code can register that exact peer-id on the shared public PeerJS server first, causing the legitimate host's Peer() to fail with 'unavailable-id' (handled at line 82) OR letting the attacker impersonate the host to guests that connect to `millos-${roomCode}`. There is no proof-of-host (signature/secret) binding the room to the legitimate creator.
src/multiplayer/SignalingService.ts.fix: Bind room ownership to a secret: host generates a random host token, derives a non-guessable peer-id (e.g. include a high-entropy suffix not shown to guests until they prove the join code), and guests verify a host-signed value in metadata. Minimum: self-host the signaling server so id allocation can be authenticated.

src/components/ui-new/widgets/VotingPane.title: Active/History tab toggle uses plain buttons without tab semantics
src/components/ui-new/widgets/VotingPane.detail: The Active/History switcher is a `<div className="flex rounded bg-slate-700/30 p-0.5">` (line 537) containing two `<button>`s (lines 538-557) styled by `activeTab === 'active'/'history'` but with no `role="tablist"` on the container, no `role="tab"` / `aria-selected` on the buttons, and the content region below (`activeTab === 'active' ? ...`, line 562) has no `role="tabpanel"`. Screen readers cannot perceive selected state or the tab relationship. Lower severity as this is a secondary in-widget toggle, not primary navigation.
src/components/ui-new/widgets/VotingPane.fix: Add `role="tablist"` to the wrapper (line 537), `role="tab" aria-selected={activeTab==='active'}` (and 'history') to each button, and `role="tabpanel"` to the list container at line 561. No visual change but it alters semantics, so verify against tests.

src/components/ui-new/panels/OverviewPan.title: Game-speed button group lacks selected-state semantics; Pause button is icon-only with only title
src/components/ui-new/panels/OverviewPan.detail: The speed controls (Pause / 1x / 10x / 60x, lines 59-105) form a mutually-exclusive selection (`gameSpeed === 0/180/1800/10800` toggles active styling) but no button exposes `aria-pressed` or radio semantics, so screen readers can't tell which speed is active. Additionally the Pause button (lines 59-69) is icon-only (`<Pause className="w-3.5 h-3.5" />`) relying solely on `title="Pause"` for its name; `title` is an unreliable accessible name. (The 1x/10x/60x buttons have visible text, so only Pause lacks a robust name.)
src/components/ui-new/panels/OverviewPan.fix: Add `aria-label="Pause"` to the Pause button (line 59) and `aria-pressed={gameSpeed === N}` to all four speed buttons (or wrap as `role="radiogroup"` with `role="radio" aria-checked` like SettingsPanel.tsx:255). aria-pressed/label is SR-only; the radiogroup conversion is semantic.

src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.title: AI decision feed has no live region; new AI decisions are not announced to screen readers
src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.detail: The decisions list at lines 307-350 renders `aria-hidden`-free but plain divs with no aria-live region. New AI decisions stream in (the panel 'now strictly visualizes the state', line 132) but a screen-reader user gets no announcement when a decision is added. The checklist calls for live regions for AI decisions. Note: putting aria-live on the whole scrolling list (307) would be noisy — the right fix is a small polite status summary that announces the latest decision action.
src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.fix: Add an sr-only `role="status" aria-live="polite"` element that updates with a concise summary of the newest decision (e.g. `New AI decision: ${decision.action}`), rather than making the entire list a live region. UX/behavior change — recommend product sign-off on verbosity.

src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.title: AICommandCenter tab switcher uses plain buttons, not ARIA tab semantics
src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.detail: The Decisions/Strategic tab switcher at lines 280-304 uses styled `<button>` elements toggling `activeTab` but lacks `role="tablist"`/`role="tab"`/`aria-selected`/`aria-controls`, and the content area (line 307) is not a `role="tabpanel"`. Compare WorkerDetailPanel.tsx:486-512 and SCADAPanel.tsx:546-635, which both implement full tab semantics. Functional but inconsistent; SR users don't get tab-position/selection feedback.
src/components/AICommandCenter.tsx.fix: Wrap the two buttons in `role="tablist"`, give each `role="tab"` + `aria-selected={activeTab===...}` + `aria-controls`, and mark the content area `role="tabpanel"` with a matching id, mirroring the existing WorkerDetailPanel pattern.

src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.title: MultiplayerPanel imported EAGERLY, pulling peerjs into the boot path
src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.detail: Line 33: `import { MultiplayerPanel } from '../panels/MultiplayerPanel';` is a static (non-lazy) import, unlike the ~18 sibling panels (lines 20-76) which all use lazy(() => import(...)). MultiplayerPanel.tsx:27 imports getMultiplayerManager from ../../../multiplayer, whose MultiplayerManager.ts:13 imports SignalingService which imports peerjs. ContextSidebar renders eagerly inside GameInterface. Result: the 'multiplayer' chunk (peerjs WebRTC) is requested on initial load for users who never touch multiplayer.
src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.fix: Make MultiplayerPanel lazy to match siblings: `const MultiplayerPanel = lazy(() => import('../panels/MultiplayerPanel').then(m => ({ default: m.MultiplayerPanel })));` and wrap its render at line 168 in the existing Suspense. Defers peerjs to first panel open.

src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.title: Version <select> controlled value 'v0.3' matches no <option> (React warning + blank/mismatched display)
src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.detail: Line 289 sets value="v0.3" on a controlled select, but the options at lines 295-297 are value="v0.30", "v0.20", "v0.10". A controlled <select> whose value matches no option triggers a React console warning and renders with no option selected (browser falls back to first option's label), so the footer version indicator is visually wrong/inconsistent with the actual deploy. The onChange also navigates window.location.href = `/${e.target.value}/` (e.g. '/v0.20/'), so the option values must match real routes; the current state means the displayed 'current version' is indeterminate.
src/components/ui-new/sidebar/ContextSid.fix: Set value to an existing option (likely value="v0.30") so the control reflects the live build; verify the option values map to real millos.net subpaths before relying on the navigation onChange.

src/workers/textureWorker.ts.title: If kept: texture worker uses structured-clone (no Transferables) and TextureWorkerManager lacks onerror/fallback
src/workers/textureWorker.ts.detail: If kept rather than deleted: worker results return ImageData via `self.postMessage({ ..., result })` (textureWorker.ts:107) with no transfer list, and the hook posts via `workerRef.current.postMessage({type,id,payload})` (useTextureWorker.ts:92) — full-copy structured clone of potentially 2048x2048 RGBA buffers (~16MB) per decode instead of transferring the ArrayBuffer. Also TextureWorkerManager (useTextureWorker.ts:151) sets onmessage but NO onerror handler and has no main-thread decode fallback if the module worker fails to construct.
src/workers/textureWorker.ts.fix: Prefer deleting (prior finding). If kept: transfer ImageData.data.buffer in postMessage transfer lists, add onerror handler + main-thread createImageBitmap fallback in TextureWorkerManager.

src/utils/texturePreloader.ts.title: 16 procedural canvas textures (up to 512x512) generated synchronously on the main thread during boot useEffect
src/utils/texturePreloader.ts.detail: preloadGenerativeTexturesOnce() runs in an App.tsx:410 mount useEffect. Though wrapped in `new Promise(resolve => {...})`, the body (texturePreloader.ts:28-53) runs all 16 generate* calls SYNCHRONOUSLY before resolve() — incl. generateConcrete(512,...) twice and generatePanelNormal(512,...). Main-thread CPU work during the first frames after mount (code measures/logs elapsed ms), adding TTI jank on low-end devices.
src/utils/texturePreloader.ts.fix: Defer to requestIdleCallback or chunk the 16 generations across animation frames so they don't block the first interactive frames. Low risk; verify magnitude before prioritizing.

vite.config.ts.title: recharts (343kB) chunk risk — verify SPCCharts/ComplianceDashboard sit behind a lazy boundary
vite.config.ts.detail: manualChunks puts recharts in a 'charts' chunk (vite.config.ts:109). ProductionMetrics (lazy via UIOverlay.tsx:74) and SCADAPanel (lazy via panelPreloader) are deferred, but SPCCharts.tsx:28 and ComplianceDashboard.tsx:43 import recharts directly. Whether the 343kB charts chunk loads on boot depends on whether these two are reachable only through a lazy boundary. If an eager parent renders them, the charts chunk loads for users who never open a chart panel. Lower confidence — needs a real build chunk-graph check.
vite.config.ts.fix: Audit the import parents of SPCCharts.tsx and ComplianceDashboard.tsx; ensure every recharts consumer is reachable only through a lazy() boundary so the 343kB charts chunk is demand-loaded.

vite.config.ts.title: loadEnv return value discarded (dead call)
vite.config.ts.detail: `loadEnv(mode, '.', '');` is called but its return value is unused. The commented-out `define` block below it (lines 75-78) once consumed it via `env.GEMINI_API_KEY`. Now nothing reads loaded env in the config, and base path is derived from `process.env.VERSION` directly (line 56). The call is a harmless no-op but is dead code left from the removed key-embedding logic.
vite.config.ts.fix: Remove the `loadEnv(mode, '.', '');` line, or assign and actually use it. No behavior change. The adjacent SECURITY comment block (lines 73-78) correctly documents why the API-key define is disabled — keep that.

src/components/TruckBay.tsx.title: Dock-equipment animations driven by ref reads in render never react to docking cycle
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.detail: WheelChock, DockLeveler, DockPlate, DockSpotter, StretchWrapMachine and WarehouseWorker receive their reactive booleans from refs that are mutated inside useFrame but read during render, e.g. line 2877 `<DockLeveler position={[0, 2, -1.5]} isDeployed={shippingDockedRef.current} />`, line 2925 `isDeployed={shippingDockedRef.current}`, line 2941 `isGuiding={ shippingStateRef.current === 'backing' || ... }`. `shippingDockedRef`/`shippingDoorsOpenRef`/`shippingStateRef` are assigned inside the useFrame loop (e.g. line 2627 `shippingDockedRef.current = truckState.phase === 'docked' || ...`). Reading `.current` during render does NOT trigger a re-render when the ref mutates. The cleanest victims are WheelChock (effect deps `[isDeployed]`, line 137) and DockLeveler (effect deps `[isDeployed]`, line 2550) — their deploy/lerp animation only re-registers when the prop value actually changes, which it never does mid-cycle. TruckBay (a non-memoized React.FC) only re-renders when its parent MillScene re-renders, and MillScene's productionSpeed prop comes from App's `useState(0.8)` slider — so re-renders are user-driven and infrequent. Net effect: wheel chocks, dock levelers, dock plates and the spotter's wave do not deploy/retract in sync with trucks docking; they stay frozen at whatever value was captured at the last unrelated re-render. The internal `isGuidingRef.current = isGuiding` in DockSpotter (line 1430) proves the author intended live reactivity.
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.fix: Lift the truck dock/door/phase state that drives equipment into React state (or subscribe the equipment to the production store dock-status values that useFrame already writes via setTruckDocked/updateDockStatus), so prop changes re-render the equipment. Minimal version: replace the docked/doorsOpen/phase refs that feed JSX props with useState updated in useFrame (throttled to state transitions only, which the code already detects). All edits stay inside TruckBay.tsx.

src/components/TruckBay.tsx.title: Shipping dock-status update nested inside `if (receivingTruckRef.current)` block
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.detail: The shipping truck's HolographicDisplay status update (lines 2775-2795, `const shippingCycle = adjustedTime % CYCLE_LENGTH; ... updateDockStatus('shipping', ...)`) is mis-nested: brace-depth analysis confirms it sits inside the `if (receivingTruckRef.current) {` block opened at line 2683 and closed at line 2796. So shipping dock status only updates when the receiving truck ref is non-null. In practice this is a no-op because both `<group ref={shippingTruckRef}>` and `<group ref={receivingTruckRef}>` are rendered as a pair and attach together, so the receiving ref is always present when shipping status needs updating. Still a genuine logic-locality bug: the shipping-status block is unrelated to the receiving truck and belongs at the useFrame top level.
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.fix: Move the shipping-status computation/updateDockStatus block (lines 2775-2795) out of the `if (receivingTruckRef.current)` block to sit at the useFrame body level alongside the receiving-status block, mirroring the symmetric structure.

src/components/TruckBay.tsx.title: Inline getTruckState arrow defeats RealisticTruck registration effect, causing register/unregister churn
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.detail: At line 3206 `getTruckState={() => getShippingState(performance.now() / 1000)}` (and line 3580 for receiving) a new arrow function is created on every TruckBay render. RealisticTruck's registration effect lists it as a dependency: line 3658 `}, [truckId, getTruckState]);`. Since RealisticTruck is a plain React.FC (not memoized) and getTruckState identity changes each render, the effect tears down (unregisterTruckComponents + clearTimeout) and re-registers (new setTimeout(checkAndRegister, 0)) on every parent re-render. Impact is bounded because TruckBay re-renders are infrequent (user-driven productionSpeed slider / isTabVisible / graphicsQuality), but it is wasteful churn of the truck animation registry and can briefly drop the truck from the animation manager during the 0ms timeout window.
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.fix: Wrap getShippingState/getReceivingState (or the inline arrows passed at 3206/3580) in useCallback so identity is stable, OR remove getTruckState from the effect dependency array at line 3658 since it is only used to register a stable accessor. Verify visually that trucks still animate.

src/components/TruckBay.tsx.title: Inline workAreaBounds object literal churns WarehouseWorker registration effect
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.detail: At line 3116 `workAreaBounds={{ minX: -8, maxX: 8, minZ: -5, maxZ: 8 }}` (and line 3536) passes a fresh object literal each render. WarehouseWorkerWithPalletJack registers the worker in an effect with `}, [workerId, isActive, workAreaBounds]);` (line 1016), so the new object identity re-runs the effect (unregisterWorker + registerWorker) on every parent re-render. Same low-but-real churn class as the getTruckState finding. Additionally `isActive={shippingDoorsOpenRef.current}` (line 3115) reads a useFrame-mutated ref in render, so the worker's active state does not reactively update (related to the dock-equipment staleness finding).
src/components/TruckBay.tsx.fix: Hoist the workAreaBounds literal to a module-level const or wrap in useMemo so identity is stable across renders; this removes the registration churn without behavior change.

src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.title: AnimatedRiverWater ignores later flowSpeed prop changes (useRef init-once)
src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.detail: `const uniforms = useRef({ ... flowSpeed: { value: flowSpeed } });` (lines 428-434) initializes the shader uniform from the `flowSpeed` prop only once. If a caller later changes flowSpeed, `uniforms.current.flowSpeed.value` is never updated (no effect syncs it; useFrame at line 534 only updates `time`). Same latent staleness applies to StillCanalWater's analogous uniforms ref. Not a live bug today because all call sites pass static flowSpeed (e.g. line 1216 `flowSpeed={1.2}`), but it is a correctness trap if a caller ever animates flowSpeed.
src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.fix: Add a small useEffect that syncs `uniforms.current.flowSpeed.value = flowSpeed` when the prop changes, or document that flowSpeed is mount-only. Low risk; keep behind owner sign-off since it touches shader visuals.

src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.title: AnimatedFrog heart key uses Date.now(), can collide on rapid pets
src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.detail: `const id = Date.now();` (line 1321) is used as the React key/id for spawned HeartParticle elements (line 1420 `key={h.id}`). Two pet clicks within the same millisecond produce duplicate keys, triggering React key collision warnings and potentially one heart's onComplete removing the wrong entry. Purely cosmetic Easter-egg path (clicking the pond frog), so impact is trivial.
src/components/FactoryExterior.tsx.fix: Use a monotonic counter ref (`heartCounter.current++`) or `crypto.randomUUID()` for the heart id instead of Date.now().

src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.title: Wonderware historian base URL hardcoded to plaintext http:// — Basic-auth credentials sent in clear
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.detail: `this.baseUrl = \`http://${this.serverHost}:${this.serverPort}/Historian/v1\`;` is hardcoded to http. The constructor builds a Basic-auth header from username/password/domain (lines 95-102: `this.authHeader = \`Basic ${credentials}\``) and `fetchWithAuth` (line 318-320) sends it on every request. With no TLS option, historian credentials (potentially Windows domain creds) traverse the network base64-encoded but unencrypted. On a millos.net (https) page this is also a mixed-content request the browser will block, so the feature silently fails AND leaks creds where it does work. There is no `protocol`/`useSsl`/`https` config field in WonderwareConnectionConfig to override it.
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.fix: Add an optional `useSsl?: boolean` (or `baseUrlOverride`) to WonderwareConnectionConfig in HistorianInterface.ts and derive scheme (`https://` default) instead of hardcoding `http://`. Defaulting to https is the safe posture for SCADA credentials.

src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.title: Wonderware adapter maps response.Data without verifying it is an array (response-shape gap)
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.detail: `const data: WWHistoryResponse = await response.json(); return this.mapWWValuesToHistoryPoints(data.Data);` (lines 166-167, 191-192, 217-218; getLatestValue line 228 maps `[data]`). No check that `data.Data` exists/is an array. A 200 response missing `Data` throws in mapWWValuesToHistoryPoints (line 332 `items.map`). Caught upstream and converted to empty result, hiding the contract violation from the user.
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.fix: Guard: `const rows = Array.isArray(data?.Data) ? data.Data : []; return this.mapWWValuesToHistoryPoints(rows);` and validate the single-value body in getLatestValue before mapping.

src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.title: getPlotValues fallback divides duration by intervals with no zero/negative guard (Infinity / negative interval)
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.detail: On TrendData failure the fallback computes `const intervalMs = Math.floor(durationMs / intervals); return this.getInterpolatedValues(tagId, startTime, endTime, intervalMs);` (lines 212-214). If a caller passes `intervals: 0` (reachable via HistorianQueryOptions.intervals), this is `durationMs / 0 = Infinity` → `resolutionMS=Infinity` in the InterpolatedData URL. If startTime>endTime, durationMs is negative → negative resolutionMS. Either produces a malformed historian request.
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.fix: `const safeIntervals = intervals > 0 ? intervals : 100; const durationMs = Math.max(0, endTime.getTime() - startTime.getTime()); const intervalMs = Math.max(1, Math.floor(durationMs / safeIntervals));`

src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.title: OPC quality mask comment says 16-bit but math assumes 8-bit; values >0xFF mis-classified
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.detail: mapOpcQuality does `const major = (opcQuality >> 6) & 0x3;` while the doc comment (lines 56-58) states 'OPC quality is 16-bit: high 8 bits = major'. For a standard 8-bit OPC DA quality byte, `(q>>6)&0x3` correctly extracts the 2-bit major field, so current behavior is right for 8-bit codes. But if Wonderware's REST API returns the full 16-bit OPC quality word (which some AVEVA endpoints do), the major bits are at >>14, and `(q>>6)&0x3` reads substatus bits instead — every value would be mis-graded. Contract ambiguity between comment and code.
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.fix: Confirm the WW REST API's Quality field width. If 8-bit, fix the comment to say 8-bit. If 16-bit is possible, normalize first: `const q8 = opcQuality > 0xff ? (opcQuality >> 8) : opcQuality; const major = (q8 >> 6) & 0x3;`

src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.title: Wonderware adapter forces plaintext HTTP, sending Basic-auth credentials in cleartext
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.detail: this.baseUrl = `http://${this.serverHost}:${this.serverPort}/Historian/v1`; is hardcoded to http:// (never https). The constructor then builds `Basic ${btoa(domain\\username:password)}` (lines 95-102) and fetchWithAuth attaches it as the Authorization header (lines 314-328). Base64 is encoding, not encryption: any on-path observer reading the cleartext HTTP request recovers the Windows/SQL domain credentials verbatim. There is no scheme option and no upgrade to TLS. This is a credential-disclosure / SCADA-network downgrade exposure.
src/scada/adapters/WonderwareAdapter.ts.fix: Default the scheme to https:// and make it configurable (e.g. add a `secure`/`scheme` field to WonderwareConnectionConfig, defaulting to https). Refuse to send Basic-auth credentials over a plain-http URL (throw, or warn-and-strip) unless the host is explicitly localhost. Mirror the same guard in any other adapter that builds a base URL.

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: REST batch read does not validate response shape before .map — malformed 200 response trips false connection-loss
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: `const data: RESTBatchResponse = await response.json(); const tagValues = data.tags.map(...)`. There is no check that `data` is an object or that `data.tags` is an array. A 200 response with an unexpected/empty body (`{}`, an array, a server returning `{error:...}`) throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'map')`. Because `readTags` is called from `readAllTags` → `poll()` (line 290) whose catch routes to `handleConnectionError()` (line 293), a single malformed-but-OK response is misclassified as a lost connection, incrementing reconnectAttempts and ultimately calling disconnect() which drops all subscribers (see separate finding).
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: After json(), guard: `if (!data || !Array.isArray(data.tags)) throw new Error('Malformed batch response');` and validate each entry has tagId/value/quality/timestamp (mirror isValidBatchTag in messageValidation.ts) before parseTagResponse. Same guard belongs on readTag's `data` at line 145.

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: After 5 reconnect failures REST adapter silently disconnects and clears all subscribers with no consumer notification
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: In handleConnectionError: `if (this.reconnectAttempts >= 5) { this.disconnect(); }`. disconnect() (lines 108-123) clears `this.subscribers` and `this.globalSubscribers`. Consumers that registered callbacks via subscribe() are never told the stream died — their callbacks just stop firing forever, and there is no error event/callback surfaced. `lastError` stays the generic 'Connection lost'. UI bound to live tags will freeze on stale data with no indication. Compare WebSocketAdapter which keeps retrying up to 10 times rather than tearing down subscribers.
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: On terminal failure, set a distinct lastError (e.g. 'Reconnect limit reached') and emit a connection-status change rather than silently clearing subscribers; or do not clear subscribers in the auto-disconnect path so a later manual reconnect can resume. Needs a product call on desired reconnect ceiling/UX.

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: poll() swallows all errors as 'connection lost' — distinguishes neither HTTP status nor parse failures
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: `private async poll() { ... try { ... } catch { this.handleConnectionError(); } }` (lines 286-295). Any throw — auth 401/403, malformed JSON, a single bad batch — is treated identically as a transient connection error driving exponential backoff. A 401 (bad API key) will never recover via reconnect and just burns 5 attempts then tears down subscribers. No status-code-specific handling.
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: In readTags/readTag surface the HTTP status on the thrown error, and in poll's catch distinguish auth/4xx (terminal, set lastError and stop) from 5xx/network (retry).

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: REST getStatistics readsPerSecond/uptime computed from connectTime even across reconnect churn; no avgReadLatency reset
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: getStatistics (lines 270-280) computes `uptime = (Date.now() - this.connectTime)/1000` and divides cumulative readCount by it. On reconnect, connect() resets connectTime (line 91) but stats (readCount/totalLatency) are never reset, so readsPerSecond is computed against a fresh uptime denominator with a stale cumulative numerator, yielding inflated rates right after reconnect. Diagnostic-only, but misleading for operators reading the stats panel.
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: Either reset the stats object in connect()/disconnect(), or track a separate `statsWindowStart` independent of connectTime so rate denominators match the counter accumulation window.

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: RESTAdapter sets Bearer token via raw fetch, bypassing secureFetch timeout/validation wrapper
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: Line 354 `headers.set('Authorization', 'Bearer ${this.config.apiKey}')` is sent via raw fetch at line 332 (`return await fetch(url, { ...options, signal: controller.signal })`). The codebase ships a secureFetch wrapper (apiSecurity.ts:512) with timeout + same-origin credentials + audit hooks specifically for this, but RESTAdapter does not use it. The Bearer key (SCADA endpoint credential) rides on whatever URL config.url points to with no allowed-host validation. Confirms the secureFetch dead-code finding from the consumer side.
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: Owner decision: either standardize RESTAdapter on secureFetch (gains timeout/audit, but changes request semantics), or document that raw fetch is intentional and remove the unused secureFetch. Adapter behavior change needs validation against the 1199-test baseline.

src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.title: Bearer token attached to any URL with no scheme/host allowlist (token leak to untrusted endpoints)
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.detail: fetchWithAuth validates only `new URL(url)` parses (line 346), then unconditionally sets `Authorization: Bearer ${this.config.apiKey}` (line 354) for every request, including to whatever baseUrl the user/config supplies. There is no check that the scheme is https or that the host matches the configured baseUrl origin. If baseUrl is http:// (allowed - no scheme restriction), the bearer token is sent in cleartext. The URL-validation comment implies a security control but it only guards against malformed URLs, not against sending credentials to an attacker-controlled or downgraded endpoint. PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth (PIAdapter.ts:338-360) has the same pattern with no URL/scheme validation at all.
src/scada/adapters/RESTAdapter.ts.fix: In fetchWithAuth, only attach the Authorization header when url.protocol === 'https:' (or host is localhost), and assert the request URL's origin matches the configured baseUrl origin before sending the token. Apply the same origin/scheme guard in PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth.

src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.title: PI adapter maps response.Items without verifying it is an array (response-shape gap → silent empty result)
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.detail: Every PI read does `const data: PIStreamValuesResponse = await response.json(); return this.mapPIValuesToHistoryPoints(data.Items);` (lines 151-152, 179-180, 205-206; getLatestValue line 219 maps `[data]` assuming the body is a single PIValue). If PI returns a body without `Items` (error envelope, partial response), `items.map` in mapPIValuesToHistoryPoints (line 363) throws. HistorianRouter.fetchFromRemote catches it (line 273) and returns `[]`, so a malformed response degrades to 'no data' with only a logger.warn — charts show empty trends and the operator gets no signal that the historian returned garbage.
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.fix: Guard each: `const items = Array.isArray(data?.Items) ? data.Items : []; return this.mapPIValuesToHistoryPoints(items);`. For getLatestValue, validate `data` is an object with a Timestamp before mapping.

src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.title: msToIsoDuration emits PT0S for sub-second intervals and malformed durations for negative/NaN input
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.detail: `const seconds = Math.floor(ms / 1000); if (seconds < 60) return \`PT${seconds}S\`;` — any intervalMs < 1000 (e.g. a 500ms interpolation request) floors to `PT0S`, which PI Web API rejects as an invalid interval. A negative intervalMs yields `PT-5S` and NaN yields `PTNaNS`. getInterpolatedValues passes this straight into the URL (line 166-171) with no lower bound.
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.fix: Clamp at the top: `const safeMs = Number.isFinite(ms) && ms > 0 ? ms : 1000;` and ensure the smallest unit is at least 1 (`const seconds = Math.max(1, Math.floor(safeMs/1000))`).

src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.title: PI getWebId assumes /points?path= returns a single PIPointResponse object; PI returns Items[] for path queries
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.detail: `const data: PIPointResponse = await response.json(); this.webIdCache.set(tagId, data.WebId); return data.WebId;` (lines 329-331). PI Web API point lookups by path commonly return either a single point or, for some endpoints, an envelope. If the body is an error/empty object, `data.WebId` is undefined and gets cached (line 330) — the undefined WebId is then cached permanently, so every subsequent read for that tag short-circuits via the cache check `if (cachedWebId !== undefined)` at line 313... note: caching `undefined` actually does NOT poison the cache (it's not set when undefined-keyed), but caching a literal undefined value via set() stores `undefined` and the `!== undefined` guard then treats a cache-miss-stored-undefined as a hit returning undefined. Net: a one-time bad lookup is cached as a permanent failure for that tag until disconnect.
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.fix: Validate before caching: `if (!data || typeof data.WebId !== 'string') { return null; }` then `this.webIdCache.set(tagId, data.WebId); return data.WebId;` so transient failures are not cached as permanent.

src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.title: PI Basic-auth credentials base64-encoded with no TLS enforcement on baseUrl
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.detail: case 'basic': const credentials = btoa(`${config.username}:${config.password}`); this.authHeader = `Basic ${credentials}`. The baseUrl is used verbatim in fetchWithAuth with no scheme check (PIAdapter.ts:338-360, no `new URL()` validation at all, unlike RESTAdapter). If a user configures an http:// PI Web API baseUrl, the Basic credentials are transmitted in cleartext. The comment at line 354 ('SSL verification is browser-controlled') acknowledges TLS is not enforced by this code.
src/scada/adapters/PIAdapter.ts.fix: Before sending Authorization, assert this.baseUrl uses https:// (or host is localhost); otherwise throw a clear configuration error. Add `new URL()` validation to PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth to match RESTAdapter's guard.

src/scada/HistorianRouter.ts.title: No time-range validation before historian queries (start>end, NaN dates, intervals=0 reach adapters)
src/scada/HistorianRouter.ts.detail: getRecordedValues/getInterpolatedValues/getPlotValues (lines 126-166) and the underlying PI/WW adapters perform zero validation that `startTime <= endTime`, that dates are valid (an Invalid Date yields `.toISOString()` throw), or that `intervals`/`intervalMs` are positive. A reversed range silently produces an empty or server-error result; an Invalid Date throws inside the adapter URL build. There is no single choke point enforcing the contract documented in HistorianInterface.ts.
src/scada/HistorianRouter.ts.fix: Add a guard at the top of getBlendedData / each public method: if either date is invalid (`Number.isNaN(startTime.getTime())`) or `endTime < startTime`, return [] (or throw a typed RangeError) before delegating. Cheap, prevents adapter-level surprises.

src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.title: Timed-out WebSocket connect still schedules background reconnects after the connect promise rejects
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.detail: On connect timeout (line 95-98) the code `reject(new Error('Connection timeout')); this.ws?.close();`. Closing the socket fires `onclose` → `handleDisconnect()` (line 134-135), and because `isDisconnecting` is still false, handleDisconnect schedules an exponential-backoff reconnect (lines 383-393). So a caller whose `connect()` rejected (and who may construct a fresh adapter and retry) now has an orphaned background reconnect loop on the rejected socket, potentially doubling connection attempts and never surfacing as connected/disconnected to the caller.
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.fix: Set `this.isDisconnecting = true` (or a dedicated `connectAborted` flag) before calling `this.ws?.close()` in the timeout handler so onclose does not start a reconnect for a connect that the caller already saw fail.

src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.title: WS batch/snapshot handler trusts t.tagId/value/quality/timestamp without per-field guards despite validator gaps
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.detail: In handleMessage, batch/snapshot does `msg.tags.map((t) => ({ tagId: t.tagId, value: t.value, quality: this.parseQuality(t.quality), timestamp: t.timestamp }))` (lines 321-327). isValidWSMessage→isValidBatchTag does validate these fields, so today it is safe, but parseQuality (line 356) calls `quality.toUpperCase()` — if a future message path bypasses the validator or quality is the empty string the result is fine, but if quality were undefined (e.g. validator relaxed) this throws. The 'update' path is safer (parseTagValue uses `?? 'GOOD'`). Inconsistency: update defends with nullish coalescing, batch relies entirely on the external validator. Low-risk today, brittle to validator drift.
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.fix: Make parseQuality null-safe (`const q = String(quality ?? '').toUpperCase();`) and apply `?? Date.now()` / `?? 0` defaults in the batch map to match parseTagValue, so the two ingest paths fail identically.

src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.title: WebSocket adapter silently downgrades https->ws (insecure) instead of wss
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.detail: const url = wsUrl.replace(/^http/, 'ws'); maps both http->ws AND https->ws? No: it maps https->wss correctly only by accident because 'https' starts with 'http' and becomes 'wsss'... actually `'https://x'.replace(/^http/,'ws')` yields 'wss://x' (correct). But a bare `ws://`/`http://` config is accepted with no enforcement of a secure scheme, so a plaintext, unauthenticated WebSocket to a SCADA proxy is allowed. The bigger gap: there is no auth on the WS connection at all (no token in the URL or first message) - any party who can reach the proxy URL can subscribe to and write tags. Writes are sent as `{type:'write', tagId, value}` (lines 216-220) over this unauthenticated channel.
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.fix: Warn or refuse when the resolved url is ws:// (non-wss) unless host is localhost. Add an auth token (from config) to the WS handshake (query param or first 'subscribe' message) so the proxy can reject unauthenticated clients. Confirm the https->wss replacement intent with an explicit scheme map rather than a regex on 'http'.

src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.title: Validated WS message tags written to value store with no tagId allowlist (unvalidated tag writes / injection)
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.detail: In the 'batch'/'snapshot' branch, msg.tags is iterated and `this.values.set(tv.tagId, tv)` stores every tag id the server (or a MITM on an unauthenticated ws://) sends, even tag ids not in this.tags (the known TagDefinition set). isValidWSMessage only checks tagId is a non-empty string, not that it is a known tag. A malicious/compromised proxy can inject arbitrary tagIds and values that then propagate to global subscribers and any UI/alarm logic keyed on tag values. The 'update' branch (line 311-317) has the same gap. Writes outbound are gated by accessMode (line 211) but inbound values are not gated to known tags.
src/scada/adapters/WebSocketAdapter.ts.fix: In handleMessage update/batch/snapshot branches, drop tag values whose tagId is not in this.tags (or whose accessMode forbids the source). i.e. `if (!this.tags.has(t.tagId)) return;` before this.values.set. Optionally clamp numeric values to the tag's engLow/engHigh.

src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.title: Other users' chat and names held in volatile memory only — good minimization, but no notice; chat is plaintext over the channel
src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.detail: Positive note for data-minimization: the multiplayer store keeps remote players, peers, and chat in in-memory Maps/arrays with no persistence — grep confirmed no localStorage/sessionStorage/IndexedDB usage anywhere under src/multiplayer or multiplayerStore.ts. addChatMessage caps history at 100 (`chatMessages: [...state.chatMessages.slice(-99), message]`) and leaveRoom()/reset() drop it. The residual privacy point: chat messages carry `fromName` (display name) and are relayed to all peers via host re-broadcast (MultiplayerManager.handleMessage CHAT case) with no content disclosure or retention notice to participants. No GDPR storage issue, but the absence of any disclosure that chat is shared in cleartext with all room peers (and re-broadcast by the host) is worth flagging.
src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.fix: Owner decision: include chat in the same pre-join disclosure ('chat is visible to everyone in the room'). No code change needed for the minimization aspect, which is already sound.

src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.title: Room codes are low-entropy and brute-forceable via PeerJS public server
src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.detail: generateRoomCode() draws 6 chars from a 32-char alphabet = ~30 bits, but the room peer-id is the predictable string `millos-${roomCode}` (SignalingService.ts:54/117). Because the host registers a guessable, namespaced peer-id on the shared PeerJS *public* cloud server, an attacker can enumerate `millos-XXXXXX` ids to discover/join active rooms (or probe which codes are live) without any invite, room password, or host approval. There is no admission control: handleIncomingConnection accepts every connection and immediately adds the peer (SignalingService.ts:134-137, MultiplayerManager.ts:136). Combined with the unvalidated-payload and intent-spoof issues, an unsolicited peer who guesses a code gets full guest authority.
src/stores/multiplayerStore.ts.fix: Add an admission step: host should require a join secret (the host-displayed code can stay short, but gate actual acceptance on a longer shared token passed in conn.metadata and verified before calling onPeerConnected/addPeer). Reject connections whose metadata token does not match. Longer term, self-host peerjs-server (already noted as a TODO in SignalingService PEERJS_CONFIG comment) and randomize the host peer-id instead of deriving it from the room code.

src/components/ui/CostEstimationOverlay..title: Floating-panel chrome drift: rounded-xl + colored borders vs design-system rounded-2xl + border-white/10
src/components/ui/CostEstimationOverlay..detail: The design-system surfaces (Dock.tsx:108, ContextSidebar.tsx:251, ShiftHandoverSummary.tsx:193) use 'border border-white/10 rounded-2xl'. The HUD floating panels diverge on corner radius and border treatment: CostEstimationOverlay.tsx:61 'rounded-xl border border-cyan-500/30', EnergyDashboard.tsx:127 'rounded-xl border border-emerald-500/30', MultiObjectiveDashboard.tsx:130 'rounded-xl border border-purple-500/30'. The colored accent borders may be intentional semantic color-coding, but the corner-radius mismatch (rounded-xl vs rounded-2xl) and white-vs-colored split are visible inconsistencies when these float alongside the sidebar/dock.
src/components/ui/CostEstimationOverlay..fix: Standardize corner radius to rounded-2xl across all floating glass panels to match Dock/Sidebar. If the accent border colors are deliberate, keep them but align radius and border-opacity; otherwise unify on border-white/10.

src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.title: Mobile panel border color differs from desktop sidebar (border-slate-700/50 vs border-white/10)
src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.detail: MobilePanel uses 'bg-slate-900/95 backdrop-blur-xl border border-slate-700/50 rounded-2xl' (line 893) while the desktop ContextSidebar uses the same bg/blur/radius but 'border border-white/10' (ContextSidebar.tsx:251). Same panel family, two different border tokens, so the mobile and desktop panel chrome read slightly differently. Same inconsistency family as the floating-panel drift above.
src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.fix: Use border-white/10 on MobilePanel to match the canonical panel border, or define one shared panel-chrome class consumed by both.

src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.title: Mobile panel content maxHeight uses a hardcoded 56px header subtraction that can mismatch actual header height
src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.detail: The panel is capped at maxHeight: '33vh' (line 888) and the scrollable content area is maxHeight: 'calc(33vh - 56px)' (line 910). The header is 'px-4 py-3' (24px vertical padding) plus icon/text line-height plus a 1px bottom border, so 56px is an estimate. If the header text wraps on a narrow device or the icon/font scales, the subtraction is too small and the content's bottom is clipped or the panel exceeds 33vh. Magic-number coupling between two siblings rather than a measured/flex layout.
src/components/mobile/MobilePanel.tsx.fix: Use a flex column (header flex-none, content flex-1 min-h-0 overflow-y-auto) inside the maxHeight:33vh container instead of subtracting a hardcoded 56px, so the content height tracks the real header height.

src/components/ui/MultiObjectiveDashboar.title: Top-left anchor collision: MultiObjectiveDashboard, ShiftHandoverSummary, and StatusHUD all stack at left-4/top
src/components/ui/MultiObjectiveDashboar.detail: Three independently-toggled overlays anchor to the top-left corner: MultiObjectiveDashboard 'fixed left-4 top-20 ... z-40' (line 130), ShiftHandoverSummary 'fixed top-16 left-4 z-50' (ShiftHandoverSummary.tsx:193), and StatusHUD default position {x:16,y:16} (StatusHUD.tsx:28). When the MultiObjective dashboard is open and a shift change fires, ShiftHandover (top-16=64px) renders directly over the dashboard's header (top-20=80px). ShiftHandover is transient (5s auto-dismiss) and on a higher z-index, so the overlap is brief, but the layout has no coordinated stacking.
src/components/ui/MultiObjectiveDashboar.fix: Offset ShiftHandoverSummary's top anchor when MultiObjectiveDashboard is visible, or move one panel to a different corner so the two cannot overlap.

src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.title: Dock lucide icons have no explicit size class - DockItem icons vs trailing-button icons may render at different sizes
src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.detail: DockItems pass bare icons (e.g. <Factory />, <Brain /> at lines 125-186) and the trailing buttons also pass bare icons (<Database /> line 202, <Eye /> line 220, <Maximize/Minimize/> line 241), none with an explicit w-/h- class - they all rely on lucide's default 24px. Elsewhere in the codebase icons are explicitly sized (e.g. MobilePanel getPanelIcon uses 'w-5 h-5'). Relying on the default means any future lucide default change, or wrapping that constrains the SVG, silently changes dock icon scale; it also makes the dock icon size implicit and inconsistent with the explicit-sizing convention used in sibling components.
src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.fix: Add an explicit size to the dock icons (e.g. size={20} or className 'w-5 h-5') for consistency with the rest of the UI and to lock the dock's visual scale.

src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.title: Dock (z-50) and MobilePanel (z-50) share the same z-index, relying on DOM order to layer
src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.detail: The Dock is 'z-50' (line 108) and the mobile slide-up panel is 'z-50' (MobilePanel.tsx:885) with its backdrop at 'z-40' (line 875). Two interactive fixed elements at the same z-index resolve stacking by DOM source order rather than explicit intent; the MobilePanel bottom is positioned above the dock via 'bottom: max(100px, ...+90px)' (line 887), so they currently do not overlap, but the equal z-index is fragile if either offset changes.
src/components/ui-new/dock/Dock.tsx.fix: Give the dock and the mobile panel distinct, intentional z-index values (e.g. dock z-50, mobile panel z-[60]) so layering is explicit rather than order-dependent.

src/components/ui-new/panels/SafetyPanel.title: Minor section-heading color drift across widgets (text-slate-400 vs text-slate-500, one tracking-wide outlier)
src/components/ui-new/panels/SafetyPanel.detail: The established section-heading convention is 'text-xs font-bold ... uppercase tracking-wider text-slate-400' (SafetyPanel.tsx:55,126,173). Across ui-new widgets/panels the survey shows the heading muted color split between text-slate-400 (12 uses) and text-slate-500 (10 uses), and one heading uses tracking-wide instead of tracking-wider. Small but visible inconsistency in the muted-heading shade and letter-spacing between adjacent panels.
src/components/ui-new/panels/SafetyPanel.fix: Pick one muted heading color (text-slate-400) and one tracking value (tracking-wider) and apply consistently, ideally via a shared SectionHeading component or class.

src/components/truckbay/useTruckPhysics..title: Truck-state functions allocate large object literals every frame (called 4x/frame)
src/components/truckbay/useTruckPhysics..detail: calculateShippingTruckState / calculateReceivingTruckState each build ~6 inline waypoint object literals (e.g. `const TUNNEL = { x: 20, z: 270, rot: Math.PI };`, APPROACH, SETUP, DOCK, PULLOUT_END, EXIT_ROAD) plus a large ~14-field return object literal (`return { phase: 'entering', x: ..., z: ..., rotation: ..., speed: ..., steeringAngle: 0, brakeLights: ..., reverseLights: false, ... }`) on EVERY invocation. They are pure functions called once per frame from each of: TruckBay.tsx main shipping useFrame (~2608), TruckBay.tsx receiving useFrame, and FactoryExterior.tsx checkpoint useFrame (~4193-4194 calls BOTH). With 2 checkpoints that is up to 4-6 calls/frame, each allocating ~7 objects = sustained per-frame GC pressure (the documented anti-pattern this lane targets). The waypoint constants are frame-invariant and could hoist to module scope; the return shape could reuse a module-level mutable state object the callers read synchronously.
src/components/truckbay/useTruckPhysics..fix: Hoist the frame-invariant waypoint literals (TUNNEL/APPROACH/SETUP/DOCK/PULLOUT_END/EXIT_ROAD and the per-style dimension tables) to module-level const. Optionally have each function mutate-and-return a single module-level TruckAnimState object instead of a fresh literal, since callers consume it synchronously within the same frame and do not retain it. Verify visually that truck animation is unchanged.

src/components/Environment.tsx.title: new THREE.Color allocated inside the consolidated lens/daylight useFrame
src/components/Environment.tsx.detail: Inside the consolidated useFrame (block '15. Update Daylight'), line 490 does `const THREEColor = new THREE.Color(color);` every time the block runs. It is throttled to every 6th frame (~10fps) so the impact is bounded, but it still allocates a THREE.Color on the render loop where the file already establishes the module-temp pattern (_cameraDir/_lightPos/_toCamera at lines 95-97). The value is then only used to feed `mat.color.set(THREEColor)`.
src/components/Environment.tsx.fix: Add a module-level `const _daylightColor = new THREE.Color();` near the existing _cameraDir temps, then replace line 490 with `_daylightColor.set(color);` and use `_daylightColor` in the forEach. Behavior identical; one fewer allocation per throttled tick.

src/components/Environment.tsx.title: getDaylightProperties returns a fresh object literal per call, invoked from 3 per-frame blocks
src/components/Environment.tsx.detail: getDaylightProperties (line 670) returns a new object literal on every branch (e.g. `return { color: '#1e3a5f', intensity: 0.1, opacity: 0.2 };`, seven such returns). It is called from 3 separate per-frame useFrame contexts in Environment.tsx (the daylight block at ~488, and two other `const { ... } = getDaylightProperties(gameTime)` call sites), each allocating a short-lived object every frame they run (some throttled, some not verified throttled). Minor but a repeated per-frame literal in the hot path.
src/components/Environment.tsx.fix: Either memoize the last result keyed on the rounded hour (the function is a pure step-function of gameTime), or have it write into a module-level reusable object the callers read. Low risk; values unchanged. Prefer flagging since it touches an exported helper used by multiple call sites.

src/components/Environment.tsx.title: THREE.Color allocated inside useFrame daylight update
src/components/Environment.tsx.detail: `const THREEColor = new THREE.Color(color);` is allocated inside the useFrame daylight block (:490). Throttled via shouldRunThisFrame(6) (≈10fps at 60fps), so ~10 Color allocations/sec — minor GC churn, consistent with the per-frame-alloc class CLAUDE.md already flags for this file but at a low rate. Reported for completeness; not launch-blocking.
src/components/Environment.tsx.fix: Hoist a module-level `const _daylightColor = new THREE.Color();` and use `_daylightColor.set(color)` then `mat.color.copy(_daylightColor)`. Behavior-identical.

src/utils/sanitize.ts.title: sanitizeWorkerName / sanitizeUrl / isUrlSafe / decodeHtmlEntities exported but never used
src/utils/sanitize.ts.detail: sanitizeWorkerName (224), sanitizeUrl (254), isUrlSafe (287), decodeHtmlEntities (57), validateObject/createStringValidator/createNumberValidator are exported via index.ts:99-115 but grep across src/ (minus def+barrel) returns ZERO consumers. These are unused sanitization scaffolding shipped to the bundle. Not a vulnerability by itself but inflates bundle and signals unfinished security wiring.
src/utils/sanitize.ts.fix: After wiring the chat/name sanitizers (the genuinely needed ones), prune the remaining unused sanitize exports from utils/index.ts or wire sanitizeWorkerName where custom worker names are accepted. Dead-code cleanup, no runtime impact.

src/components/terrain/TerrainGround.tsx.title: Terrain geometry (+ splatMap/heightmap DataTextures) leak GPU memory on every graphics-quality change
src/components/terrain/TerrainGround.tsx.detail: geometry is built in useMemo with deps [width, height, segments, heightmapData, riverConfig.depth]: `const geometry = useMemo(() => { if (heightmapData) { return createDisplacedGeometry(...) } return new THREE.PlaneGeometry(width, height, 1, 1); }, [width, height, segments, heightmapData, riverConfig.depth]);`. In MillScene.tsx terrainResolution/terrainSegments/terrainEnableRiverChannel all derive from graphicsQuality (lines ~716-728), and GraphicsSettingsPanel.tsx calls setGraphicsQuality at runtime. So toggling quality recreates the geometry (and the splatMap at :108 and heightmap at :114, both THREE.DataTexture GPU resources). The geometry is attached via `<primitive object={geometry} attach="geometry" />` (:152). R3F does NOT auto-dispose objects passed via <primitive> — it doesn't own them — and there is zero dispose() call anywhere in src/components/terrain/. Each quality change orphans the prior GPU vertex buffer + 2 DataTextures. Not tick-driven so it is not a continuous leak, but it is an unbounded leak across repeated user quality toggles.
src/components/terrain/TerrainGround.tsx.fix: Add a useEffect cleanup that disposes the previous geometry/textures, e.g. capture geometry/splatMap/heightmap in refs and on change/unmount call geometry.dispose(), splatMap.dispose(), heightmapTexture.dispose(). Simplest: `useEffect(() => () => geometry.dispose(), [geometry]);` plus equivalents for the splatMap and heightmap DataTextures. Verify visually after a quality toggle (3D, not test-covered).

src/components/terrain/TerrainMaterial.t.title: TerrainMaterial MeshStandardMaterial created in useMemo is never disposed
src/components/terrain/TerrainMaterial.t.detail: `const material = useMemo(() => { const mat = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({...}); ... return mat; }, [...])` (component form at :264, hook form useTerrainMaterial at :344 with deps []). The material is returned and rendered into the terrain mesh. There is no dispose() in the file. The component-form useMemo at :264 carries shader-injection deps, and the parent TerrainGround remounts/recomputes on graphics-quality change, so the prior MeshStandardMaterial (with its compiled custom shader program from onBeforeCompile at :285/:352) is orphaned on quality toggle. Compounds the TerrainGround geometry/texture leak.
src/components/terrain/TerrainMaterial.t.fix: Add `useEffect(() => () => material.dispose(), [material]);` in both useTerrainMaterial and the component path so the GPU program/material is released when it is replaced or the terrain unmounts. Visual regression risk is low (dispose only fires on replacement/unmount) but it is a 3D path — confirm terrain still renders after a quality toggle.

src/components/VillageArea.tsx.title: Module-level villageCobbleMaterial uses onBeforeCompile without a customProgramCacheKey
src/components/VillageArea.tsx.detail: `villageCobbleMaterial.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => { ... }` injects custom vertex/fragment chunks but no `villageCobbleMaterial.customProgramCacheKey` is set. Three.js's documented severe variant of this bug (Date.now() in the key) is already fixed in TerrainMaterial; here the risk is the inverse — a default cache key means this shader-modified material could share a compiled program with another MeshStandardMaterial of identical parameters and render without the feathering injection. No observed collision in this scope (it is a single module-level singleton used once at :1750), so this is precautionary, not a reproduced defect.
src/components/VillageArea.tsx.fix: Add a stable explicit key: `villageCobbleMaterial.customProgramCacheKey = () => 'villageCobble_feather_v1';` to guarantee the injected variant gets its own compiled program. No runtime UX change.

src/components/mobile/MobileFirstPersonC.title: Two THREE.Vector3 allocated per frame inside useFrame (mobile movement)
src/components/mobile/MobileFirstPersonC.detail: Inside the useFrame body: `const forward = new THREE.Vector3(0, 0, -1).applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion);` and `const right = new THREE.Vector3(1, 0, 0).applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion);` (lines 215-216). These run every frame the player is moving on mobile, allocating 2 Vector3 per frame → GC pressure on the lowest-powered target devices. Gated behind movement (early return at :206 when direction length is 0), so impact is bounded to active-movement frames — minor, not launch-blocking.
src/components/mobile/MobileFirstPersonC.fix: Hoist to module-level reusable vectors (the pattern already used elsewhere in this codebase per CLAUDE.md, e.g. SmartForklift/Environment fixes): `const _forward = new THREE.Vector3(); const _right = new THREE.Vector3();` then `_forward.set(0,0,-1).applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion); _right.set(1,0,0).applyQuaternion(camera.quaternion);` Behavior-identical.
